title: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The moment a user or team crosses a product-specific usage threshold that predicts sustained retention. For Slack, activation is defined as a team exchanging 2,000 messages.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: A B2B go-to-market motion in which a product enters organizations through individual teams at a price point not requiring senior management approval, spreading organically before formal enterprise procurement begins.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: Paul Buchheit's framework stating that excellence in a few critical dimensions is more valuable than broad adequacy across many features. Named for the creator of Gmail.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The go-to-market challenge faced by products in markets where prospective customers do not yet recognize the problem the product solves, requiring the company to establish category legitimacy before selling its solution.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: Slack's organizational unit combining QA and customer support into a single team responsible for processing feedback, routing it to product teams, and resolving user issues — treated as a strategic asset rather than a cost center.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The number of unique users engaging with a product on a given day, measuring genuine engagement distinct from total registered accounts or teams created.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The plan a company executes to bring a product to market, encompassing positioning, pricing, distribution, launch tactics, customer acquisition, and retention.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: Slack's cross-device synchronization feature that tracks exactly where each user left off in every conversation across all devices in real time, eliminating context loss when switching between devices.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: A product-specific activation threshold, determined empirically from cohort retention data, at which a team's probability of long-term retention becomes highly predictable. Slack's magic number is 2,000 messages, after which 93% of teams remain active.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The go-to-market activity of teaching prospective customers that a product category exists and that they have the problem it solves — required when a significant portion of the target market does not recognize the category.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: A product category that is new or undefined, in which customers have not yet established buying criteria, recognized solution providers, or standard evaluation processes, requiring buyer education before persuasion.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: A framing choice for a controlled early-access product release that signals quality and intentionality, as opposed to calling the same release a "beta," which connotes instability and incompleteness.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: Non-numerical user insights gathered through support tickets, social media mentions, interview responses, and open-ended feedback — treated as equally important to quantitative metrics.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The rate at which users or teams continue using a product over time after activation. Slack's 93% retention rate among teams crossing the 2,000-message threshold was the business case for investing in activation.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: The dynamic in collaborative software where a single team member who refuses to use the product can prevent the entire team from adopting it, making it qualitatively different from individual-user product adoption.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: Slack's curated collection of enthusiastic, unsolicited public tweets from users expressing extreme satisfaction — used as social proof and a symbol of organic word-of-mouth advocacy, many originating from resolved customer service complaints.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B

cards.definition: Organic product awareness and adoption driven by users spontaneously recommending or publicly praising a product to their networks, without paid promotion. Slack's primary growth mechanism, enabled by exceptional product quality and customer service.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Slack Launch Strategy — From 0 to $1B
